channel.title: Ben Ofe's Sports Data Blog
channel.description: A website about sports and data

item.title: How Sam Darnold turned around his career
item.dc:creator: Ben Ofe
item.description:
Sam Darnold was the third overall draft pick in the 2018 NFL Draft by the New York Jets. A guy who had very high expectations coming out of the draft, being picked before guys like Josh Allen and Lamar Jackson. Unfortunately, Darnold did not live up to those expectations.

Three miserable years with the Jets, time with the Carolina Panthers that he would much rather forget about, and a backup role with the San Francisco 49ers in 2023 that he was handed. This had most NFL fans ready to give up entirely on the former third-overall draft pick. It couldn’t get any worse for Darnold, until Kevin O’Connell called and ultimately helped Darnold revitalize his career from being a guy written off to now a franchise quarterback for the Seattle Seahawks.

Code 
library(tidyverse)
library(ggrepel)
library(ggalt)


passing25 <- read_csv("2025NFLPassing.csv") |> mutate(Season = 2025, PlayerSeason = paste(Player, Season))

alltimepassing <- read_csv("nfl_qb_passing_19752025.csv")|> mutate(PlayerSeason = paste(Player, Season))


goodqb <- alltimepassing |>
  filter(PassingYds > 2500, TD >= 15)

career <- alltimepassing |> 
  group_by(Player) |> 
  arrange(Season) |> 
  mutate(
    CumulativePassingYards = cumsum(PassingYds)
  ) |>
  ungroup() |> 
  filter(Season > 2015) 

Darnold <- career |>
  filter(
    Player == "Sam Darnold")

Elite <- career |> 
  filter(Player == "Tom Brady" | Player == "Patrick Mahomes" | Player == "Cam Newton" | Player == "Andrew Luck" | Player == "Aaron Rodgers") 

elitelabel <- Elite |> 
  group_by(Player) |> 
  filter(Season == max(Season))

ggplot() + 
  geom_step(data=career, aes(x=Season, y=CumulativePassingYards, group=Player), color="grey", alpha=.4) +
  geom_step(data=Darnold, aes(x=Season, y=CumulativePassingYards, group=Player), color="#69BE28", linewidth=2) +
  geom_step(data=Elite, aes(x=Season, y=CumulativePassingYards, group=Player), color="red") +
  geom_point(data=elitelabel, aes(x=Season, y=CumulativePassingYards), color="red")+  
  geom_text_repel(data=elitelabel, aes(x=Season, y=CumulativePassingYards, label=Player))+
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = c(2016,2018,2020,2022,2024,2025), limits = c(2016,2025))+
  labs(
     title = "Sam Darnold is closing the gap in yards on NFL greats",
     subtitle = "From being benched to now currently being one of the leagues most prolific passers.",
     x = "",
     y = "Cumulative Passing Yards",
     caption = "Source: Pro Football Reference | By Ben Ofe"
     ) +
  theme_minimal() + 
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10), 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot"
    )

The cumulative yards is where the entire narrative of him restoring his career begins as a whole. You can see that his line was near the bottom of the NFL statistically while QB’s like Brady and Rodgers were at the top of the league in this category. There was a long stint in the line that it stayed almost nearly flat and didnt change much up until 2024. Where you can see Darnold’s line shoot straight up. This goes to show how Darndold in the last two years has made a huge jump and brought his career back on track.

Code 
Mahomes <- career |>
  filter(
    Player == "Patrick Mahomes")

Hurts <- career |>
  filter(
    Player == "Jalen Hurts")
  
Maye <- career |>
  filter(
    Player == "Drake Maye") 

Stroud <- career |>
  filter(
    Player == "C.J. Stroud")
  
Rodgers <- career |>
  filter(
    Player == "Aaron Rodgers")

AverageQBR <- career |> 
  group_by(Season) |> summarise(mean_QBR=mean(QBR, na.rm=TRUE))


ggplot() +
  geom_line(data=Darnold, aes(x=Season, y=QBR),color="#69BE28") +
  geom_point(data=Darnold |> filter(Season == max(Season)), aes(x=Season, y=QBR, group=Player), color="#002244") +
  geom_line(data = Mahomes, aes(x=Season, y=QBR), color= "#E31837")+
  geom_point(data= Mahomes |> filter(Season == max(Season)), aes(x=Season, y=QBR, group=Player), color="black") +
  geom_line(data = Hurts, aes(x=Season, y=QBR), color= "#A5ACAF")+
  geom_point(data= Hurts |> filter(Season == max(Season)), aes(x=Season, y=QBR, group=Player), color="grey") +
  geom_line(data = Maye, aes(x=Season, y=QBR), color= "#002244")+
  geom_point(data= Maye |> filter(Season == max(Season)), aes(x=Season, y=QBR, group=Player), color="black") +
  geom_line(data = Rodgers, aes(x=Season, y=QBR), color= "#FFB612")+
  geom_point(data= Rodgers |> filter(Season == max(Season)), aes(x=Season, y=QBR, group=Player), color="black") +
  geom_line(data = AverageQBR, aes(x=Season, y=mean_QBR), color= "black")+
  geom_point(data= AverageQBR |> filter(Season == max(Season)), aes(x=Season, y=mean_QBR), color="black") +
  geom_text(data=Darnold |> filter(Season == max(Season)), aes(x=Season + 1, y=QBR +.1, group=Player, label=Player)) +
  geom_text(data=Mahomes |> filter(Season == max(Season)), aes(x=Season + 1.2, y=QBR +.1, group=Player, label=Player)) +
  geom_text(data=Maye |> filter(Season == max(Season)), aes(x=Season + 1, y=QBR +.1, group=Player, label=Player)) +
  geom_text(data=Rodgers |> filter(Season == max(Season)), aes(x=Season + 1, y=QBR +.1, group=Player, label=Player)) +
  geom_text(data=Hurts |> filter(Season == max(Season)), aes(x=Season, y=QBR -5, group=Player, label=Player)) +
  geom_text(aes(x=2020, y=48, label = "Average")) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = c(2016,2018,2020,2022,2024,2026), limits = c(2016,2027))+
  labs(
     title = "Sam Darnold's resurgence in QB Rating",
     subtitle = "From one of the worst QB Ratings in the NFL to one of the most improved.",
     x = "",
     y = "QB Rating",
     caption = "Source: Pro Football Reference | By Ben Ofe"
     ) +
  theme_minimal() + 
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10), 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot"
    )

As you can see for years here that Darnold was below the average QB rating while the other top NFL quarterbacks remained steady above the line and near the top. In his 2020 season with the New York Jets he reached a career low but has been trending upwards ever since then. He has recently surpassed the leagues averaged QB rating and improved vastly within the last few years. This goes to show lasting progress and that Sam Darnold wasn’t a one year wonder.

Code 
currentqb <- alltimepassing |>
  filter(PassingYds > 1000, TD >= 5, Season > 2020)

lowendqb <- currentqb |>
  filter(PassingYds > 2000, TD >= 20, Season > 2020)

midqb <- currentqb |>
  filter(PassingYds >= 3000, TD >= 30)

topqb <- currentqb |>
  filter(PassingYds >= 4500, TD >= 42)

qblabels <- bind_rows(topqb, Darnold)

ggplot() + 
  geom_point(data=currentqb, aes(x=`Cmp%`, y=`Y/G`), color="grey")+
  geom_point(data=lowendqb, aes(x=`Cmp%`, y=`Y/G`), color="grey")+
  geom_point(data=midqb, aes(x=`Cmp%`, y=`Y/G`), color="grey", alpha=.3)+
  geom_point(data=topqb, aes(x=`Cmp%`, y=`Y/G`), color="grey")+
  geom_point(data = Darnold,aes(x=`Cmp%`, y=`Y/G`), color="#69BE28")+
  geom_text_repel(data=qblabels, aes(x=`Cmp%`, y=`Y/G`, label=PlayerSeason))+
  labs(title="Darnold has flipped the script in the last two years", 
       subtitle="After years in the bottom half, Darnold's last two seasons put him among the most productive passers.",
       x="Completion Percentage",
       y="Yards per Game",
       caption = "Source: Pro Football Reference | By Ben Ofe") +
  theme_minimal() + 
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10), 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot"
    )

The scatterplot reflects how in the first six years of his career he was consistently in the bottom left of the graph due to poor play that led to a bad completion percentage and a low average yards per game. That right there tells the whole story of how he was struggling to be an efficient quarterback during that time. When in 2024 and 2025, his dots appear in the top right half of the graph. Sitting alongside names like Matthew Stafford and Patrick Mahomes who are seen as some of the top quarterbacks in the NFL.

I may be a little biased when it comes to it, but I think that Sam Darnold’s story is one of the better comeback stories to exist in the NFL. There was no reason for any coach or GM to believe in him after his first six years in the league putting up below average numbers with three different franchises but he bought in and proved the doubters wrong and that got him to where he is today with Mike Macdonald and the Seattle Seahawks. Where he just recently won a SuperBowl and has a three-year, $100 million contract.

item.title: Why Jaxon Smith-Njigba is the most efficient WR in the NFL
item.dc:creator: Ben Ofe
item.description:
High-volume receivers in the NFL aren’t usually the most efficient when it comes to it. Throw a guy the ball 150 times he is obviously going to put up some numbers. It’s about what they do with it once they catch the ball. Players like Justin Jefferson, DK Metcalf, and Courtland Sutton struggled this season to be efficient at high volume.

That was the exact opposite story for Jaxon Smith-Njigba. He wasn’t the highest volume receiver in the NFL, but nobody came close to what he could do with once he got the ball. JSN also wasn’t just the NFL receiving yards leader but the most efficient wideout this past season of the National Football League.

Code 
library(tidyverse)
library(ggrepel)
library(ggalt)

Reclogs <- read_csv("2025NFLRec.csv")

receivers <- Reclogs|>
  filter(Pos == "WR", Tgt >= 100) |>
  group_by(Player) |>
  mutate(
    catch_rate = Rec / Tgt
  ) |> ungroup()

top20 <- receivers |> 
  top_n(15, wt=catch_rate) |>
  arrange(desc(catch_rate))

JSN <- top20 |>
  filter(
    Player == "Jaxon Smith-Njigba"
  )
ggplot() +
  geom_bar(
    data=top20, 
    aes(x=reorder(Player, catch_rate), weight=catch_rate), fill="grey") +
  geom_bar(
    data=JSN, 
    aes(x=reorder(Player, catch_rate), weight=catch_rate),
    fill="#69BE28"
    ) +
    labs(
     title = "Jaxon Smith-Njigba is among the leagues best in hauling in catches",
     subtitle = "The Seahawks star receiver is top 3 in the NFL in catch rate behind two all-pro wideouts.",
     x = "",
     y = "catch rate",
     caption = "Source: Pro Football Reference | By Ben Ofe"
     ) +
  coord_flip() + 
  theme_minimal() + 
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10), 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot"
    )

Efficiency for a receiver isn’t just about the total yardage that they get. Jaxon Smith-Njigba has developed into a very reliable target for Seahawks quarterback Sam Darnold, thanks to his elite separation and ability to make difficult catches look easy. One of the key statistics that highlights this is his catch rate percentage amongst other top receivers in the NFL. Smith-Njigba, who is top 3 in the NFL in catch rate behind two all-wideouts, makes him a key part in keeping drives and plays alive.

Code 
middle <- Reclogs |>
filter(Pos == "WR", Tgt >= 50) 

toprec <- Reclogs |>
  filter(Pos == "WR", Tgt >= 125, Yds >= 1200)

ggplot() + 
  geom_point(data=Reclogs, aes(x=Tgt, y=Yds), color="grey") +
  geom_point(data=middle, aes(x=Tgt, y=Yds), color="black")+
  geom_point(data=toprec, aes(x=Tgt, y=Yds), color="red") +
  geom_point(data = JSN, aes(x=Tgt, y=Yds), color="#69BE28") + 
  geom_text_repel(data=toprec, aes(x=Tgt, y=Yds, label=Player))+
  geom_encircle(data=toprec, aes(x=Tgt, y=Yds), s_shape=.5, expand=.03, colour="red") +
  geom_text(aes(x=115, y=1300, label="Top Receivers")) + 
  labs(title="Smith-Njigba does more with less compared to the rest", 
       subtitle="Compared to other wide receivers in the NFL the Seattle Seahawks receiver outputs more yards on less catches.",
       x="Targets",
       y="Receiving Yards",
       caption = "Source: Pro Football Reference | By Ben Ofe") +
  theme_minimal() + 
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 18, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10), 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot"
    )

As you can see in the chart, Jamar Chase, Amon Ra St. Brown, and Puka Nacua led the league in volume, but Jaxon Smith-Njigba maximized every opportunity that came his way. He, in turn, added more production this year on less receptions than any other top receiver in the league at the position, this year showing again how efficient he is. That is what efficiency is all about doing more with less.

Code 
recvtargets <- Reclogs|>
  filter(Pos == "WR", Tgt >= 150) |>
  group_by(Player) 


ggplot() + 
  geom_segment(
    data=recvtargets, 
    aes(
      y=Player, yend=Player,
      x= `Y/R`, xend= `Y/Tgt`)
  ) + 
  geom_point(
    data=recvtargets,
    aes(
      y=Player,
      x=`Y/R`,
      color="Yards per Reception"
    ), size=2
  ) + 
  geom_point(
    data=recvtargets,
    aes(
      y=Player,
      x=`Y/Tgt`,
      color="Yards per Target"
    ), size=2
  ) +
  scale_color_manual(name="Receiving", values=c("#002244","#69BE28")) +
labs(title="A look at how the top NFL WRs turn Receptions into Yards", 
       subtitle="Jaxon Smith-Njigba leads the top receivers in the NFL with yards after catch.",
       x="Yards per Target vs Yards per Reception",
       y="",
       caption = "Source: Pro Football Reference | By Ben Ofe") +
  theme_minimal() + 
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold"),
    axis.title = element_text(size = 8), 
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size=10), 
    panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
    plot.title.position = "plot"
    )

Now this is where JSN excels and does his dirty work here. The green dot is yards per target that shows the yardage of a ball thrown the way caught or not, and the navy dot is what he averages when he makes a catch. You can see on this dumbbell chart that Smith-Njigba leads in the NFL with his 15.1 yards per reception in this stat by a WIDE margin. Before he even catches the ball, you can see that he almost already has all-pro guys like Jamar Chase and Chris Olave beat with what they do on each reception. This is largely in part due to his ability to take the short and medium yardage catches and get upfield to turn them into something more.

The common denominator that comes up here in every chart is that JSN accomplishes more with less than other receivers in the NFL. He catches everything that Sam Darnold throws his way, plays with a ton of efficiency, and makes things happen after the catch. That’s exactly what makes him the most effective wide receiver.
